title: Shared Account Gaming Purchase Dispute After Prolonged Usage Pattern
narrative: Cardholder disputes $89-$299 gaming platform purchases (Steam, Xbox Live, PlayStation Network) 45-90 days post-transaction, claiming unauthorized use by household member. Pattern emerges when ProductCD='C' (digital content) transactions show successful account login history and continued platform usage for 30-60 days before dispute filing. Transaction occurs during cardholder's typical usage hours (txn_hour matches 80%+ of historical pattern), same device fingerprint as previous legitimate purchases, and P_emaildomain matches cardholder's primary email domain on file. Chargeback filed precisely at merchant's dispute window closing (typically day 58-62 for digital goods). Card continues normal usage pattern for other merchants throughout dispute period. Represents exploitation of digital goods' delayed delivery verification and bank dispute timelines.
indicators: Single gaming/digital content transaction $89-$299 disputed 45-90 days post-purchase
P_emaildomain matches cardholder's verified email domain on 3+ prior transactions
txn_hour falls within cardholder's normal activity window (Â±3 hours of 70%+ transactions)
Device fingerprint (DeviceInfo hash) matches 5+ previous successful transactions same card
Chargeback filed day 58-65 post-transaction (near merchant policy deadline)
Card shows 8+ successful transactions at other merchants during dispute investigation period
dist1 value indicates billing/shipping address match (dist1 < 50km or null for digital goods)
summary: A cardholder disputes a gaming platform charge 45-90 days after purchase, claiming a family member made it without permission, but the transaction used the same device, email, and time-of-day patterns as the cardholder's previous legitimate purchases. The dispute is filed precisely near the merchant's deadline window while the card continues normal spending elsewhere.
reasoning: The cardholder likely authorized or enjoyed the digital content for weeks, then filed a dispute near the deadline to get a refund while keeping the game access. The matching device, email, timing, and continued card use all point to buyer's remorse disguised as unauthorized use rather than actual fraud.
indicator_explanations: Single mid-value gaming purchase disputed two months after delivery and usage
Email matches cardholder's verified address used on multiple legitimate purchases
Transaction occurred during cardholder's typical shopping hours across transaction history
Device has completed several successful purchases with this exact card previously
Dispute filed strategically close to merchant's refund policy expiration window
Cardholder continues using card normally at other merchants during dispute
Delivery address matches billing or transaction is digital with no location mismatch
